title: Mask Economics Shape High-NA EUV Adoption
gist: An expert roundtable (D2S, Micron, HJL Lithography, Synopsys, Lam Research) on how rising photomask costs and high-NA EUV requirements shape chipmakers' node, design, and process choices: masks rarely kill a node outright but increasingly steer layout and EUV-layer decisions, while high-NA's reduced depth of focus forces new resists (metal-oxide, Lam's dry Aether resist), thinner absorbers, SRAF-size limits, and unsolved two-mask stitching review.

insights.claim: EUV reduced mask counts versus the 193nm era because EUV can do in single patterning what 193nm requires double or triple patterning to achieve; mask counts had exploded to over 100 in some cases before EUV arrived around the 7nm era.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: EUV's mask-count reduction is the core economic argument for adopting it at the leading edge; it reframes EUV's high tool cost as a net mask-cost saver versus quad-patterning.
insights.quote: EUV can do in single patterning what 193nm requires double or even triple patterning to do.

insights.claim: The lithography market has bifurcated: many fabs deliberately specialize in non-leading-edge nodes (28nm or above) because plenty of profitable electronics never need EUV.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The trailing-edge specialization thesis supports a durable two-tier foundry market, validating mature-node capacity investments alongside the EUV leading edge.
insights.quote: There are plenty of electronics you can do at 28nm. You don't even have to go down to 14nm.

insights.claim: AI-driven GPU demand is the financial engine funding continued leading-edge litho investment; Jensen Huang said at a recent GTC he can see $1 trillion of GPU orders, up from $500 billion six months earlier.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: A doubling of visible GPU order pipeline to $1T underwrites the capex case for high-NA EUV; AI compute demand is the demand floor for the entire leading-edge equipment chain.
insights.quote: At a recent GTC, Jensen Huang said he can see $1 trillion worth of GPU orders, up from $500 billion six months ago. That's what will continue to fund this.

insights.claim: A single high-NA EUV machine is projected to cost $350 million per unit, which the speaker frames as worthwhile given AI compute economics.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The $350M per-tool figure sets the capital barrier for high-NA adoption, concentrating leading-edge litho among the few foundries (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) that can amortize it over AI-scale volumes.
insights.quote: The high-NA machine is projected to cost $350 million per unit. That's extraordinary, but it's obviously worth it.

insights.claim: Masks are rarely the sole reason a chip is not built, but mask cost is always a consideration in design and EUV-layer decisions, and the industry must keep driving mask costs down.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Mask cost acts as a continuous design constraint rather than a hard gate, meaning cost-reduction in mask making is a persistent value lever across the leading-edge ecosystem.
insights.quote: it's not common for masks to be the sole reason for not doing something.
insights.locator: Scheid, on mask economics influencing design

insights.claim: EUV mask economics are sensitive to product margin: mask cost matters far less for a leading-edge HPC product than for a very low-margin product, so litho/node decisions are application-specific.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Margin-dependent mask tolerance means high-NA adoption will concentrate first in high-ASP HPC/AI silicon and lag in cost-sensitive segments, shaping where equipment demand lands.
insights.quote: When you're dealing with a leading-edge HPC product, the cost of a mask is going to be less important than if you're dealing with a very low-margin product.

insights.claim: Even very high-budget, low-volume applications face mask-cost pressure; upgrading the electronics of the entire 19-aircraft B-2 bomber fleet would require only ~19 chips plus replacements, so mask amortization is impossible.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The B-2 example shows volume, not budget, drives mask amortization, explaining why ultra-low-volume strategic chips stay on older nodes regardless of funding.
insights.quote: there are only 19 B-2 bombers in the world. If you want to upgrade the electronics on the entire fleet, you're talking about 19 chips plus replacements.

insights.claim: Mask economics have already shaped DRAM and 3x-nm logic layout, with cost pressures driving DRAM layouts that combine periphery and array imaging on a single mask and designers optimizing pitches and layouts to maximize mask reuse.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Layout optimization for mask reuse is already an established design discipline, indicating mask-cost-aware EDA and DTCO tooling is a structural, not speculative, demand driver.
insights.quote: where cost pressures have driven layouts that combine periphery and array imaging on a single mask.
insights.locator: Wise, on mask economics shaping design

themes.theme: ILT, curvilinear masks and multi-beam writers

insights.claim: Once multi-beam writers become commonplace, EUV curvilinear masks will cost only slightly more than Manhattan masks, and using ILT can shrink the number of masks per chip by moving from multi-patterning to single patterning.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: ILT plus cheap curvilinear masks can lower total chip cost by cutting mask count, a tailwind for ILT/computational-litho tooling (Synopsys, D2S) and a cost-down lever for foundries.
insights.quote: The technology itself can actually reduce the total chip cost by reducing the number of masks needed for a given chip.
insights.locator: Fenger, on curvilinear masks and ILT

insights.claim: High-NA EUV is expected to enhance existing mask errors and drive tighter specs across local CD, EPE, local CDU, and higher resolution, plus new specifications such as mask stitching.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Tighter high-NA specs across CD/EPE/CDU expand demand for advanced mask metrology, inspection, and computational correction tools as the node transitions.
insights.quote: We'd certainly expect high-NA to enhance any existing mask errors and drive tighter specs across the board, from local CD to EPE, local CDU, and so forth, including higher resolution.

insights.claim: High-NA EUV is expected to produce stronger 3D mask effects, requiring thinner absorbers, possibly different multilayers, and even new substrate materials able to handle higher heat loads.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Mask-blank material evolution (thinner absorbers, new substrates, higher heat tolerance) opens new requirements for mask-blank and materials suppliers as high-NA ramps.
insights.quote: we have to drive the mask blank materials further — thinner absorbers, possibly different multilayers.

insights.claim: Synopsys says the major new EUV effects (anamorphic magnification, polarization, mask 3D) have largely been solved, but high-NA represents a step-function increase in required model accuracy as margins shrink.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: If core high-NA modeling problems are largely solved, the remaining accuracy demand is incremental — a continuing but manageable revenue stream for computational-litho EDA leaders.
insights.quote: High-NA is going to represent a step function in terms of the requirements for accuracy.

insights.claim: High-NA stitching creates a mask-review gap: producing a stitched wafer image requires loading two masks simultaneously, but no current tool can load two masks to create one wafer image, raising unresolved defect-qualification questions.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: A concrete unsolved tooling gap in two-mask stitching review is a potential bottleneck and a product opportunity for whoever builds the missing dual-mask emulation tool.
insights.quote: right now there's no tool that can load two masks and create one wafer image. I see that as a potential gap.

insights.claim: Mask 3D effects, once considered nearly intractable, may be turned into an advantage rather than a limitation by tailoring materials, per Fraunhofer's Andreas Erdmann (2026 SPIE Frits Zernike Award), and RIT's Bruce Smith is re-examining the decade-old molybdenum-silicon multilayer for high-NA.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: A shift from fighting mask 3D effects to exploiting them, plus fresh scrutiny of Mo-Si multilayers, points to materials-led innovation as the next high-NA enabler worth tracking.
insights.quote: Can you do better with these masks because of the mask 3D effects, rather than despite them?

themes.theme: SRAFs, depth of focus and resist limits

insights.claim: High-NA's reduced depth of focus is governed by k2*lambda/NA2, where the squared NA term is the problem, forcing the use of small sub-resolution assist features (SRAFs) on the mask.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: The NA-squared depth-of-focus penalty is the root constraint driving high-NA's hardest material and resist challenges; it explains why SRAF and resist innovation are gating items.
insights.quote: Depth of focus is dictated by the equation, k₂*λ/NA². It's the square that's the problem, and depth of focus is known to become an issue with high-NA.
insights.locator: Fujimura, on SRAFs and depth of focus

insights.claim: Required SRAF sizes may need to be as small as 15nm in mask scale, but today's fast mask-writing resists cannot reliably handle 15nm with acceptable variation, making resist speed — not the multi-beam writer — the likely limiting factor.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Resist speed-versus-resolution is the real high-NA bottleneck at ~15nm SRAFs; companies solving fast high-resolution mask resists (and metal-oxide resists) gain leverage in the high-NA enablement chain.
insights.quote: It's going to be the resist, and how fast the resist is, that will be the limiting factor.
insights.locator: Fujimura, on SRAF size and resist

insights.claim: Writing high-NA masks may require writing a full reticle twice (two masks per layer), so multi-beam writer throughput and beam count are critical; the number of parallel beams is increasing each generation.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: Doubled write time from two-mask high-NA layers makes multi-beam writer throughput a key cost variable; rising beam counts are the industry's answer and a demand driver for advanced mask-writing tools.
insights.quote: if you have to write a full reticle, and write it twice because there are two masks for high-N/A, write time matters.

insights.claim: Lam Research argues high-NA's reduced depth of focus tightens resist thickness and profile control, driving adoption of metal-oxide photoresists with higher etch selectivity and new co-optimized etch/film technologies, partly mitigated by Lam's 3D-engineered Aether dry photoresist.
insights.speaker: Gregory Haley
insights.so_what: High-NA shifts value toward dry-resist, etch, and film co-optimization — directly favoring Lam Research's Aether dry-resist and deposition/etch franchise as differentiated high-NA enablers.
insights.quote: the reduced depth of focus inherent to high‑NA systems can be partially mitigated through innovations such as Lam's 3D‑engineered Aether dry photoresist.
